planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average, well-aerated loamy soil in full sun; giant sequoia requires excellent drainage and will not tolerate waterlogged soil or clay. Plant in a permanent site — moving established trees is extremely difficult.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, deep, fertile to average loamy soil in full sun; giant sequoia is the largest living thing on Earth by volume — the named individuals in Sequoia National Park include General Sherman (at 52,500 cubic feet the largest), General Grant, and many other titans. Native to a narrow band of the western Sierra Nevada of California at 4,000–8,000 feet elevation, where it grows in moist, deep soils fed by snowmelt. The reddish-brown, deeply furrowed, fire-resistant bark (up to 3 feet thick on old trees) is one of the tree's most distinctive features. Seeds are tiny (1/4 inch long) but grow surprisingly rapidly for such a massive tree. Young trees are surprisingly suitable for large-property planting in zones 5–8 where it can reach impressive size within decades. The round, egg-shaped cones are only 2–3 inches long — remarkably small for the world's largest tree. A deeply awe-inspiring species worthy of every large garden.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, moisture-retentive, fertile loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; coast redwood grows naturally in the fog belt of the California and Oregon coast where summer fog provides constant atmospheric moisture. It grows best in moist, cool, deep soils.

directions: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, deep, moisture-retentive, fertile loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; coast redwood is the world's tallest living tree — the tallest known individual (Hyperion in Redwood National Park) reaches 380 feet. Native to the fog belt of the California and Oregon coast, coast redwood is uniquely adapted to harvest water from fog directly through its needles, supplementing root-absorbed water in the dry California summer. Remarkably fast-growing for such a large tree — growth of 3–5 feet per year is common in ideal conditions in zones 8–10. Distinct from giant sequoia in being taller but much slimmer, with flat needles arranged in two ranks (like yew), and in its ability to sprout vigorously from the base after cutting or fire. Widely planted in California, the Pacific Northwest, and milder parts of the world (Ireland, New Zealand, the UK). Cutting-propagated plants from named selections are more reliable for garden planting than seed-grown trees.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moisture-retentive, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; Japanese cedar prefers moist, well-drained, humid conditions and grows well in the Southeast where summers are warm and humid. Avoid excessively dry or alkaline sites.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moisture-retentive, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; Japanese cedar (sugi) is Japan's national tree and most important timber tree, used in shrines, temples, and traditional architecture throughout Japan. The fragrant, reddish-brown wood is prized for its durability and beautiful grain. In gardens, the species has given rise to hundreds of cultivated varieties ranging from dwarf, globose, or prostrate forms for rock gardens to tall, columnar forms for screening. The straight species grows into a large, pyramidal tree with attractive, spirally arranged, bright green to blue-green foliage that turns bronze in cold weather. Particularly well-suited to the humid Southeast (zones 7–9) where it performs better than many other conifers. The cultivars 'Elegans', 'Yoshino', 'Black Dragon', and 'Radicans' are widely used in Southeast American gardens.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moisture-retentive, fertile to average soil in full sun to partial shade; Japanese umbrella pine is slow-growing and should be planted in a permanent site where it will have room to develop its distinctive layered form. Avoid alkaline soils or dry sites.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic, moisture-retentive, fertile to average soil in partial shade to full sun; Japanese umbrella pine is among the most distinctive and beautiful conifers in cultivation — a botanical 'living fossil' with no close living relatives, placed in its own family (Sciadopityaceae). The leaves are unique: elongated, dark, lustrous, deep green 'needles' (actually modified stems called cladodes) arranged in tight whorls of 20–30 around each stem node, creating the umbrella-like appearance that gives the tree its common name. The effect is deeply sculptural — each horizontal branch tier holds a perfect whorl of glossy, umbrella-like foliage clusters. Very slow growing (6–12 inches per year), making it a long-lived architectural accent plant that takes decades to reach impressive size. The glossy foliage does not fade or bronze in winter. One of the most sought-after conifers by conifer collectors and garden designers for its uniquely dramatic and elegant silhouette.

planting.note: Plant acorns (which take two years to mature) in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; European turkey oak is one of the fastest-growing oaks and adapts to poor, dry soils where other oaks fail. Common name refers to Turkey (the country) where it is widespread, not the bird.

directions: Plant acorns in fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to lean, sandy or loamy soil in full sun; European turkey oak is a fast-growing, large deciduous oak native to southern Europe and western Asia, named for its abundance in Turkey (not the bird). It produces deeply and variably lobed, bristle-tipped leaves with variable shape, and its most distinctive feature is the acorn cup covered in long, outward-curling, mossy-looking scales — creating some of the most ornamental acorn cups in the genus. The acorns require two full growing seasons to mature, a characteristic of the red oak group. A tough, adaptable tree for poor, dry soils where English oak would fail, commonly used in Europe for woodland planting on difficult sandy or chalk soils. The fall color is variable — yellows and bronzes. Note that in the southeastern US the name 'turkey oak' refers to the native Quercus laevis, an entirely different species.
